responseSet.title: The ‘Other 23 Hours’: Special Problems of Psychotherapy in a ‘Special’ Hospital
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The ‘Other 23 Hours’:1 Special Problems of Psychotherapy in a ‘Special’ Hospital1

Sameer Sarkar MD

Sameer Sarkar

Berks RG45 7EG
e-mail: sameer.sarkar@wlmht.nhs.uk

Sameer Sarkar  

In this paper, I describe and discuss the complexities of being a therapist in a maximum security forensic psychiatric hospital, working with patients who have committed acts of serious violence, and who also suffer from severe mental illness and personality disorders. I suggest that profound disturbances in the patient's inner world get played out in triangular disputes between the patient, the therapist and other professionals working in the hospital. I also describe how real events in the life of the institution impact on the therapeutic process, and the importance for the therapist of reflection on the ‘other 23 hours’ that the patient lives outside of therapy. Finally, I raise some ethical dilemmas that arise for therapists working in such settings, where the stated therapeutic aim is not only treatment but also the containment and prevention of future violence.

From Toxic Institutions to Therapeutic Environments; Residential Settings in Mental Health Services edited by Penelope Campling, Stefan Davies and Graeme Farquharson (Royal College of Psychiatrists, 2004). 286 pp. £25.00.

Kevin Healy

Kevin Healy

Clinical Director, Cassel Hospital
Member General Psychiatry Section, APP

Kevin Healy  

Psychoanalytic psychotherapy within the public sector is increasingly challenged by the ‘evidence based mentality’, and by current exciting

98

opportunities to influence practice in various mental health settings. Windows of opportunity have arisen in relation to Department of Health Guidance on ‘Personality Disorder; No Longer a Diagnosis of Exclusion’ (National Institute for Mental Health in England (2003) Personality Disorder: no longer a diagnosis of exclusion. Policy implementation guidance for the development of services for people with personality disorder (London: Department of Health)) ‘Women's Mental Health; into the Mainstream’ (Department of Health (2002) Women's Mental Health: Into the Mainstream: Strategic Development of Mental Health Care for Women (London: Department of Health)) and ‘Organising and Delivering Psychological Therapies’ (Department of Health (2002) Organising and Delivering Psychological Therapies (London: Department of Health)).

These documents are both supportive of the psychological therapy approach, and challenging of psychological therapies in delivering this approach. Psychoanalytic psychotherapy must not be left behind.

This book, edited by three psychodynamically minded editors, Penny Campling, Stefan Davies and Graeme Farquharson sets out to help create therapeutic environments for people in mental health settings. It aims to provide an introductory text, accessible to all staff to encourage them, to excite them and to prevent them ‘reinventing the wheel’ within their services. The authors aim to position residential environments as one of a range of options to meet the needs of mental health patients and not necessarily as a last resort. The authors are to be congratulated. They meet these challenges head on and succeed admirably in producing a lively, informative and challenging multi-authored book.

How can the Association of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy learn from this book? How can individual psychoanalytic psychotherapists position themselves in relation to their patients, to their colleagues within the multidisciplinary team, and within the services where they work? The General Psychiatry section of the APP has for some years been preoccupied with many of the issues highlighted in this book. At conferences and workshops they have explored ‘Psychodynamic Formulation’, ‘Risk Assessment’, ‘The Anxieties of becoming a Consultant’, ‘The Acute Ward in a Changing Environment: Therapeutic or Counter Therapeutic?’, ‘Suicide Enquiries and How to Survive Them’, ‘Making Sense of your Psychiatric Experience’ and ‘Psychotherapy and General Psychiatry: Bridging the Gap’.

BOOK REVIEWS

Between Psychotherapy and Philosophy: Essays from the Philadelphia Association

edited by Paul Gordon and Rosalind Mayo. (London, Whurr, 2004). 180 pp. £19.50

Bertrand Russell (1946: 14), in the introduction to his History of Western Philosophy writes: ‘To teach how to live without certainty, and yet without being paralysed by hesitation, is perhaps the chief thing that philosophy, in our age, can still do for those who study it’. I think that something similar might be said of psychotherapy.

The title of this book, Between Psychotherapy and Philosophy is intriguing, raising immediate questions about the audience for whom it is written and the location of the territory to be described.

The editors provide some answers in their introduction. They explain that it is a testament to the survival of the Philadelphia Association and a celebration of its breadth of thought and engagement with philosophy. They offer a brief history of the Philadelphia Association since its foundation by Laing and others amidst the radical culture of the 1960s.

They express vexation at their continuing experience of being misunderstood or misrepresented. The editors explain that they choose to define themselves by what they are not. They highlight their involvement with philosophical thinkers, especially those from ‘the phenomenological tradition’, listing among others, Heidegger, Wittgenstein and Sartre. They note with, I think, further asperity, that the Philadelphia Association has always advocated the importance of engagement with philosophy while this has only been taken up with recent enthusiasm by other psychotherapists. They describe offering a thoughtful approach that questions pre-existing assumptions rather than adhering to a specific model or ‘dogma’.

Robin Cooper's opening chapter was first given as a paper in 1995 at the Philadelphia Association's thirtieth anniversary conference. In ‘What we take for granted’, he recounts the Philadelphia Association's sceptical philosophical stance of the importance of knowing that one does not know, referring to Montaigne and Heidegger, both of whom influenced Laing.

responseSet.title: The Use of ‘Case Study’ in Psychotherapeutic Research and Education
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Use of ‘Case Study’ in Psychotherapeutic Research and Education

Dennis Greenwood and Del Loewenthal

Del Loewenthal

Holybourne Avenue
London
SW15 4JD
e-mail: D.Greenwood@Roehampton.ac.uk
D.Loewenthal@Roehampton.ac.uk

Del Loewenthal  

This examination of a ‘Case Study’ approach to psychotherapy research originates from a study focusing on the potential for therapy with a person diagnosed with dementia who was resident in a care setting. The paper begins by looking at a contrast between idealism and realism in order to identify the epistemological basis for a critique of the claims made by Freud for psychoanalysis in relation to research.

The difference between philosophical realism and idealism are considered in relation to a scientific method of research. The arguments outlined in this paper suggest that much of what scientists claim to be statements or definitions of what is actually in the world are no more than their opinion. If Freud's case studies are similarly judged in terms of ‘opinion’, much of the criticisms directed at his case study method become far less significant. Despite Freud's claims that psychoanalysis was associated with the realism of science the case study approach advocated by him could be seen to have more of a grounding in idealism, where knowledge can be seen in terms of describing possibilities rather than in the pursuit of a definitive truth.

The work of Husserl and Heidegger are examined in relation to Freud's approach to case studies and a phenomenological perspective is considered that provides a basis for valuing ‘opinion’ in terms of an approach to research. The use of case study as a legitimate descriptive method in psychotherapeutic research is further considered by presenting a phenomenological hermeneutic case study method utilizing clinical material.

Emotional Development in Psychoanalysis, Attachment Theory and Neuroscience: Creating Connections edited by Viviane Green (Hove and New York, Brunner-Routledge, 2003). 248 pp.

Kay Goddard

Kay Goddard

Dearne Valley CMHT
16 School St, Darfield
Barnsley, S73 9ET

Kay Goddard  

While psychoanalysis has generated hypotheses but has not been hypotheses testing the converse could be said of a scientific approach which does not address questions of subjective experiences, complex affects and motivations

(p. 2).

The above statement appears in the book's introductory chapter. It offers a challenge to the commonplace view that the ‘objective’ evidence-based sciences and the ‘subjective’ field of the humanities and arts are in a dichotomous rather than a dialectical relationship with one another. This former view is particularly pervasive in the contemporary arena of NHS mental health. In this evidence-based culture, it is well documented that psychoanalytically oriented therapy has found itself in a weaker position than for example CBT, whose effects are more easily measured. The qualitative ‘subjective’ data of psychoanalytic work, interpreted from within a framework that recognizes the power of the unconscious, has been difficult to translate into measurable ‘facts’ that can speak to a wider audience. Does this then mean that the data observed, experienced and collated over the whole process of an intensive therapy is not ‘scientifically’ produced or is irrelevant to science?

‘Emotional Development in Psychoanalysis, Attachment Theory and Neuroscience’ is a timely and significant work, which moves beyond these dichotomies, through a dialectical process, into the realm of integration. It brings to the fore two rich canons of understanding about the growth of the self and what aids or undermines human development. This is something which psychoanalytically informed practice has needed for a long time, ever since Freud began ‘The Project for a Scientific Psychology’ (1895), which was never actually published in his lifetime, and which was finally abandoned, due to the lack of available techniques for studying brain functioning. However, over the last decade, technological advances in brain scanning: EEG, imaging and mapping, have helped neuroscience to flourish. This current project succeeds in bringing together the latest findings of neuroscience and clinical practice, and making clear the links between each field of discovery. Its importance lies not only in the successful linking of specialized areas of understanding and knowledge, but also in the clear scientific evidence it provides for locating emotional life in the very bedrock of biology and brain development.

Private Theories and Psychotherapeutic Technique

Björn Philips , Andrzej Werbart and Johan Schubert

Johan Schubert

Björn Philips
Björngårdsgatan 25
SE-118 52 Stockholm, Sweden
e-mail: bjorn.philips@sll.se

Johan Schubert  

The aim of this study is to explore the importance, to the therapeutic process, of the relation between the patient's and the therapist's problem formulations and private theories of pathogenesis and cure. Four cases of young adults in psychoanalytic psychotherapy were compared, two with unequivocally positive and two with more ambiguous outcome at termination. The patients and therapists were interviewed about their private theories initially and at termination of therapy, and a qualitative comparison was made between the cases. In the two more successful cases the therapists had early in therapy perceived obstacles for the therapeutic work in the patients’ ways of thinking, feeling, and relating, and made interpretative interventions focusing on these. This was not observed in the less successful cases. In the more successful cases the patient's and the therapist's private theories were more similar at termination than initially, whereas the opposite development was found in the less successful cases. One hypotheses generated is that the therapeutic process can be facilitated by a therapist listening to the patient's private theories, making interpretative interventions focusing on obstacles to the therapeutic work, including contradictions between their private theories, and monitoring the patient's reactions to these interventions.

[[RunningHead]]

Contributing to A Treatment Trial of Group Therapy: ‘Psychotherapy Supervisor’ or ‘Therapeutic Consultant’?

Adrian Sutton

Adrian Sutton

195 Hathersage Road
Manchester M13 0JE, UK
e-mail: Adrian.Sutton@cmmc.nhs.uk

Adrian Sutton  

This paper describes how psychodynamic approaches to understanding therapeutic groups and organizations were applied in a randomized control trial investigating the effectiveness of a group treatment programme for adolescents who had repeatedly attempted suicide. The treatment programme was developed by therapists using a cognitive behavioural framework. The author was invited to contribute specifically as supervisor of the group treatment, although his clinical practice is based in psychoanalytic theory. Despite the differing theoretical foundations a close collaboration developed in relation to the therapeutic work and the process of conducting this within a research project. The psychodynamic perspective simultaneously supported and challenged the author's position, the therapists’ approach and the research process.

A key aspect of the evolution of the author's role related to points of potential conflict between therapeutic and research processes. Exploration of the interrelationship of authority and the ability to fulfil responsibilities within both therapy and research was essential in supporting the therapists in ensuring that the clinical work was paramount. The role was identified as ‘consultation’ as opposed to ‘supervision’. It is suggested that differentiated roles of ‘Research Design Consultant’ and ‘Therapeutic Consultant’ should be part of psychotherapy research trials, with the latter role providing an additional safeguard in relation to ethical issues.

DANGEROUS LIAISONS: PSYCHOSIS AND VIOLENCE – WORKING IN A PSYCHIATRIC INTENSIVE CARE UNIT

Paul Terry

Paul Terry

Paul Terry  

This paper describes work with psychotic and violent patients which led to thoughts about the use of projective identification in violent enactments to communicate at physical rather than emotional levels. The excessive reliance on projective identification in these patients seems to reflect early problems in containment and to be associated with underlying fears of annihilation expressed in a dynamic oscillation between a dread of separateness and a dread of closeness. A violent enactment may attempt to resolve overwhelming anxieties about separateness by an attempt at fusion, which then may stir anxieties about engulfment. Alternatively, overwhelming anxieties about closeness may lead to violent attempts at separateness which then stir fears of fragmentation.

Moments of therapeutic contact with these patients seem to be achieved from a painful recognition of separateness but which simultaneously allows real closeness. For therapist and patient such moments seem to reflect the capacity to tolerate a third perspective, very difficult for patients whose fathers were often absent or ineffective. The therapeutic working through to achieve an integrated and separate identity is especially difficult for these patients because of problems of mourning separateness and of unravelling complex webs of projective identification which attempt to bind a fragile identity together against fears of fragmentation and death.

The Ethical Attitude in Analytic Practice edited by Hester McFarland Solomon and Mary Twyman (London & New York, Free Association Books, 2003). 183 pp. Paperback £18.99.

Adrian Sutton

Adrian Sutton

195 Hathersage Road
Manchester

Adrian Sutton  

Having been asked to facilitate a seminar on ‘Ethical Thinking’, I found myself wondering, ‘Is there such a thing as unethical thinking?’ Essentially, ethics is about the examination of our conduct rather than our thinking — are we behaving reasonably and ensuring that we maintain ourselves in the service of the patient as agreed within the process of negotiating a treatment contract? Yet, in (psycho)analytic practice, of whichever school, a large part of the activity in the service of the patient is not observable behaviour. We examine ourselves, our thoughts and feelings, to ascertain what this may tell us about our patients. Crucially, we must ensure that the internal activity of being with and processing their impact upon is properly translated in offering interpretations, formulating questions, making treatment decisions or engaging in other observable activity. For example, we must ensure that evoked feelings of hatred are contained and processed, not enacted nor denied: in that sense one might suggest that there could only be ‘unethical not-thinking’ — but how sustainable is even that position when we consider concepts such as attacks on the capacity to think? The use of counter-transference has been referred to as a ‘nodal point of clinical practice and ethical debate wherein the professionals themselves are the subjects’ (Sutton 1997). When we apply psychoanalytical principles in any area of mental health, we place ourselves in the firing-line — it is a very personal professional issue. Assistance in acquiring, using and maintaining the tools of ethical analysis for this process is welcome and essential.

The Ethical Attitude in Analytic Practice brings together papers by practitioners from both the psychoanalytic and analytical psychology schools on the basis that the aspirations and expectations of ethically sound practice are shared, fundamental principles. The book considers theory and applied issues across a breadth from individual therapy/analytic treatment, supervision, training and practice within organizations through to the dilemmas in presenting practice in a wider setting, including publication of clinical material.

[[RunningHead]]

CONSCIOUSNESS-RAISING AND WELL-BEING IN PUBLIC SPHERE ORGANIZATIONS: A HISTORICAL REVIEW OF PSYCHOANALYTICAL APPROACHES

Gary Winship

Gary Winship

Gary Winship  

This paper traces a lineage of ‘organizational depth-psychology’ from Freud through to post-Bion perspectives on organizational consultancy. In reviewing the psychoanalytic approach to organizations two dominant strands emerge; first, that theories seem to be constructed around a critical conception of institutions and, second, that the active process of organization al consultancy, deriving from this critical base, is inclined to consciousness raising through the disruption of familiarity in the culture of the organization. The challenges of de-stabilization in the process of organizational progress, survival and decay are considered.

[[RunningHead]]

On Aggression Turned against the Self

Anthony Parfitt

Anthony Parfitt

24 Combe Park
Bath BA1 3NR
e-mail: Anthony.Parfitt@awp.nhs.uk

Anthony Parfitt  

Deliberate self-harm is a common presenting problem suffered by young people. Several patients referred to the NHS outpatient clinic where I work have been able to stop harming themselves during intensive psychoanalytic psychotherapy. In this paper, I shall discuss a variety of motives for persistent cutting by one adolescent girl. I am most interested to show how the healthier part of her personality overcame her motives for harming herself, and eventually allowed her to make use of my understanding of a sadomasochistic internal object that worked against us.

Attachment Trauma: Working with Dissociative Identity Disorder and Multiplicity edited by Valerie Sinason (Brunner-Routledge, Hove, East Sussex; Taylor & Francis, New York, 2002). 280 pp.

Judith Issroff

Judith Issroff

27a Hampstead Hill Gardens, London NW3 2PJ
e-mail: judith_issroff@madasafish.com

Judith Issroff  

Like the topic of dissociation itself, chapters comprising this book vary in quality and style, and do not form an integrated or comprehensive whole, but the variety of contributions make for compelling reading that informs and enriches one in original and unexpected ways. So it is an important collation of views. One emerges with considerable respect for the diverse authors who have had the courage to grapple with people whose lives and selves have been shattered and fragmented through complex, cumulative human-generated traumata, at times originating within families of victims-witnesses and also perpetrators, of extreme, perverse, sadistic and criminal nature. Accordingly, understandably, these deeply damaged people present their varieties of defensive personalities/alternate selves to therapists in ways that challenge the credulity, quickness of response, cunning in ‘therapeutic holding’ and emotional robustness of any professional or other who encounters such a personality compendium-person.

Such encounters are inevitably difficult, including for those who themselves have previously encountered their like, and scepticism is an easy way out — as with deniers of the Holocaust: even reading is not easy. To deal with but one ‘patient’ client is demanding, to be forced to deal with several in the form of but one certainly demands ‘nothing short of everything’, including flexibility in therapeutic responsiveness and the development of new coping strategies.

I treated the book itself like a smorgasbord — except eventually, gradually I ate everything, so to speak, of this great feast. Valerie Sinason's own chapter I chose to read last. I would recommend it to be read first by those who are relatively unfamiliar with such clientele because of the vivid graphic description of a first encounter with a ‘many in one’ victim-witness-perpetrator-adult-child girl-boy-woman, which was handled by a vituoso assessor in her own inimitable style. Clearly a therapist who can accept the endurance test of such a case has to be extremely resilient, robust and creative in responsiveness: rather like a juggler, several voices have to be kept in mind, and although overtly but one person is in the room, ‘the [imaginary] group under the couch’ — as adolescent psychiatrist Fritz Redl called it — has constantly to be kept in mind. However, in these cases the ‘imaginary’ suddenly appear and different personae really do emerge and switch.

SETTING UP A COUNSELLING SERVICE FOR PARENTS, INFANTS AND YOUNG CHILDREN

Maria Pozzi and Beverley Tydeman

Beverley Tydeman

Beverley Tydeman  

In this paper a brief model of working psychotherapeutically with children under 5 years of age and parents is explored. The psychoanalytic principles that guide the work are looked at and the practical aspects of setting up a service within NHS clinics is considered. The authors trace the history of this model, its aims, technique and the use of telephone counselling for parents. They discuss referrals, the setting, the structure and content of this counselling using clinical vignettes.

[[RunningHead]]

PURE GOLD OF PSYCHOANALYSIS OR PURE CHEMISTRY? HOW CAN WE HANDLE THE COUNTER-TRANSFERENCE AND THE PSYCHOANALYST'S SUPER-EGO CONFLICTS IN COMBINED TREATMENT?1

Michael Günter

Michael Günter

Michael Günter  

The use of drugs during psychoanalysis as well as the psychoanalytical treatment of patients who need psychopharmacological medication raise a whole series of problems that are also the subject of controversial discussion. Nevertheless, the body of literature available on this topic is still quite small. Medication is invariably an outside parameter that is introduced into the psychoanalysis and it can cause a blind spot in the psychoanalytic treatment. On the other hand, as we are treating severely disturbed patients, we are confronted with a growing necessity to introduce medication into the psychoanalytic setting. The paper discusses the related conflicts of the psychoanalyst and shows in a case report how an analytic stance can go hand in hand with adequate medication. The paper argues that we have to accept the state of the art in psychiatry and at the same time continue to talk about medication in terms of psychological processes even though it poses a conflict and is also a threat to our narcissistic equilibrium.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

BECOMING ANOREXIC: ON LOSS, DEATH AND IDENTIFICATION AND THE EMERGENCE OF ANOREXIA NERVOSA

Sarah Huline-Dickens

Sarah Huline-Dickens

Sarah Huline-Dickens  

This paper investigates the connections between death, pathological identifications and anorexia nervosa. Literature is reviewed concerning various psychodynamic models of causation of anorexia, and in particular transgenerational patterns in the evolution of this eating disorder, with reference to problems in the development of autonomy of the emerging self. The role of loss in the family is examined, and the development of pathological identifications is described. A case of an adolescent girl initially presenting with an unexplained seizure is described in which multiple identifications seem to be occurring while the patient develops anorexia, perhaps reflecting previous losses in the family and also in a kind of strange identification with, and mimesis of, the parents' unmourned dead. It is suggested that the development of these multiple identifications gives some aetiological clues to this puzzling illness and also indicates a therapeutic focus. It is argued that a psychodynamically-informed child psychiatrist can bring an enriching approach to working with patients with this disorder.

Damian Gamble

Damian Gamble

8 Fitzjohns Avenue, London, NW3 5NA
e-mail: dgamble@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Damian Gamble  

New Clinical Realms consists of ten diverse chapters, organized into five sections of two chapters each: ‘Unexplored Problems’, ‘Unrecognized Personalities’, ‘Uncelebrated Partners’, ‘Unfamiliar Processes’ and ‘Untapped Potentials’. The first chapter, entitled ‘Mental Pain’, begins with a review of psychoanalytic thinking about the nature of the phenomenon. Akhtar outlines the evolution of the concept in Freud's writing from drive theory (trauma due to a breach in the protective shield) to object relations (pain due to object loss) and further development by later analysts. His summary, that mental pain is a complex phenomenon with many roots (he lists seven) will not surprise many clinicians.

[[RunningHead]]

Untying the Knot: Working with Children and Parents by A. H. Brafman (Karnac Books, London, 2001). 166 pp. £19.99.

Rosemary Loshak

Rosemary Loshak

62 Roman Road, London E2 0PG

Rosemary Loshak  

That this slim, modest volume has recently been reprinted so soon after its original publication date is testament to the wisdom contained within it and to its worth at a time when more children are experiencing emotional distress and needing professional help than ever before. The variety of different professionals working with children in different settings is now vast and parents seeking help are likely to be confused. Dr Brafman makes the point that the diagnostic evaluation and the subsequent treatment that the child receives will depend entirely on the conceptual framework of the professional who is consulted. His framework is unequivocally psychoanalytic and his book will do much to demystify this approach for parents, and for those working in child and adolescent mental health. It describes in everyday language how psychoanalytic thinking can help us to understand children's emotional difficulties. It is an application of psychoanalysis to the often necessarily brief encounters with young children and their parents, that typically occur in child guidance settings. Dr Brafman is concerned with the impact of early experience, and with unconscious fantasy upon the internal world of the child, and he aims to create understanding at a deep level which can produce change. Such change is made possible by the child's wish for help, conveyed by his presence and by the symptom which is troubling him and from which he seeks relief.

The knot is a word borrowed from Winnicott, who used it to describe an impediment, an obstacle occurring against the background of normal development. Brafman draws extensively on Winnicott's writings about consultations with children. Where he differs from Winnicott is in seeing the knot as an entanglement in which parents and child are caught up together, as the interaction between them somehow compounds the problem. This can happen when a parent's anxious response to their child is determined by unconscious factors belonging to their own early experience. It may happen also in those situations where the problem is more readily seen as one belonging to the parent.

Culture and Psychotherapy: A Guide to Clinical Practice edited by Wen-Sing Tseng and Jon Streltzer (Psychiatric Press, Washington & London, 2001). 313 pp. £36.95.

Eating Disorders and Cultures in Transition edited by Mervat Nasser, Melanie Katzman and Richard Gordon (Taylor & Francis, Hove, 2002). 224 pp. £18.99.

Eia Asen

Eia Asen

38, Marlborough Place, London NW8 OPJ

Eia Asen  

Mental health professionals nowadays inevitably find themselves treating clients of widely varying backgrounds. A culture-sensitive approach demands the therapist's curiosity about, if not familiarity with, the cultural practices of any client, couple or family. Conducting culturally appropriate and relevant psychotherapy is challenging, and understanding and respecting cultural diversity surely are a ‘must’ for any psychotherapist. Yet, how many training institutes pay more than lip service to teaching culture-sensitive practices? The occasional ‘ethnic hour’, slipped into an already cramped timetable of lectures and seminars, hardly addresses the complex cultural issues many of our clients struggle with and into which we therapists talk.

Culture can be defined as a system of shared meaning, the result of socially transmitted attitudes, beliefs and feelings that shape behaviours, organize perceptions and label experiences. Culture affects communication styles, gender and family roles, and personal and group identity. It also affects the identification and diagnosis of problems, as well as the expectations of service users and health professionals. In this sense culture constructs our identities and behaviours, while at the same time we construct culture. In most societies, (subcultures exist within cultures and usually in hierarchical power relationships, with minority cultural groups finding themselves marginalized and/or discriminated against.

In their very readable Culture and Psychotherapy, Tseng and Streltzer point out that, while there is some overlap between the terms ‘culture’, ‘race’ and ‘ethnicity’, they are nevertheless quite different. ‘Culture’ refers to a set of beliefs, value systems and behaviours that are patterned early in life and become an internal mode of regulating emotion and action. ‘Ethnicity’ is a term used for a group of people who share common cultural features. ‘Race’ tends to describe a group of people that is characterized by certain physical features.

EDITORIAL

Maureen Marks

Maureen Marks

Maureen Marks  

I am pleased to bring to your attention Paul Terry's paper, Dangerous liaisons: Psychosis and violence – working in a psychiatric intensive care unit, which was this year's winner of the Annual Clinical Essay Prize, awarded by the Educational Trust of the APP, and is published in this issue. Terry describes his therapeutic work with potentially violent patients and his understanding of their violence as an attempt to manage overwhelming states of annihilation anxiety. The sometimes frightening clinical situations he presents and his psychoanalytic understanding of these encounters illustrate very well the containment for both patient and clinician that psychoanalytic thinking can provide.

This was the third year of the Essay Prize. To give you a sense of the breadth of papers which have won so far, last year's winner was Liv Darling's paper on psychoanalytically informed work with interpreters, in a child and adolescent mental health department, and in 2003, Ian Thurston's paper about developing a therapeutic alliance in an acute day hospital. In general, the papers which have been submitted for the prize have covered a wide range of public service, and have been of a high standard. It has been difficult each year to chose one paper over others, but most have subsequently been published in the journal.

The closing date for next year's Essay Prize is 31 December 2005 and the winner will be announced at the 2006 Annual General Meeting of the APP (details about how to submit are inside the back cover of this issue). I would like to take this opportunity to encourage APP members to finish that paper which you have drafted or presented, or which you may have constructed only in your mind, and submit it for the Essay Prize. I look forward to the very great pleasure I and the other members of the judging panel, and eventually our readers, will get from reading it.

Maureen Marks

Editor

Maureen Marks

Maureen Marks  

When I came to write the editorial for this, my first issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, I found myself wondering about the origins of the Association for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy (APP) in the NHS, and I spoke with some of the people who had been involved. As with psychoanalysis itself, the APP and the Journal owe their beginnings and ongoing development to the passion of individuals. According to John Steiner, the ‘moving spirit’ behind the setting up of the APP was Mike Michalacopoulos. At that time most Consultant Psychotherapists were medical doctors and psychoanalysts. Mike Michalacopoulos told me that his concern was that there were moves (now realized) to create consultant psychiatrist posts with a special interest in psychotherapy, i.e. posts that could be filled by individuals who had not undergone a formal training in psychotherapy. He therefore wanted Consultant Psychotherapists who were analysts to play a full role in the NHS, especially in the education and training of mental health professionals. In 1979 he convened a meeting of all Consultant Psychotherapists who were analysts, they formed a committee, and Mike Michalacopoulos was elected chair. In 1981 this arrangement was formalized by setting up the APP, and its council, with John Steiner as chair, Mike Michalacopoulos as secretary and Colin James as treasurer and a very successful training scheme, which lasted for six years, was established at Queen Mary's University Hospital. Senior analysts taught on the course — Hannah Segal, Anton Obholzer, John Steiner, Edna O'Shaunessy, to name some — and it attracted both medical and non-medical mental health staff.

The council was also keen to establish a journal and Michael Sinason, at the time a Specialist Registrar in the Psychotherapy Unit at the Maudsley Hospital, and a candidate at the Institute of Psychoanalysis, came to mind. Michael Sinason had already had some experience in publishing. As a medical student he had been the editor of the newspaper of the National Association of Health Students (described by Michael, now, as a ‘student socialist gang’). At its height, this newspaper, Health Team, evolved into a national paper, backed by the BMA, with a circulation of 100,000. He had also, during 1977-79, been editor of the Bethlem and Maudsley Gazette, a ‘very energetic magazine then’.

[[RunningHead]]

Modernization or Privatization? the Future of the NHS and Implications of Governmental Reforms for Psychotherapy Services11

Marco Chiesa

Marco Chiesa

Richmond, Surrey, TW10 7JH
e-mail: m.chiesa@ucl.ac.uk

Marco Chiesa  

In this paper I argue that close attention should be paid to the changes that have been taking place in the National Health Service over the last 15 years as a result of governmental initiatives. The identification of the direction that the recent health reforms have been taking should allow us to draw significant conclusions regarding the possible fate that the NHS is facing in the not-too-distant future. The introduction of the internal market in health care, the Private Finance Initiative, private Diagnostic & Treatment Centres and Foundation Trusts may indicate that the privatization of the NHS has begun in earnest. The relevance of such considerations to all NHS workers and users needs to be appreciated, as it may go some way towards answering the important question of what kind of NHS we will be having and using in the future. The implications for the NHS in general, and for psychotherapy services in particular, of the insidious but invasive process of privatization inherent in the ongoing changes in the structure and funding of the NHS are discussed.

Aplying Psychoanalytic Thinking in A Staff Support Group to Reflect on Service Change and Clinical Practice in A Specialist Psychiatric Service

Flavia Morante

Flavia Morante

24 Kynance Mews
London SW7 4QR
e-mail: ftmorante@hotmail.com

Flavia Morante  

This paper describes how the nursing staff of a specialist psychiatric service used psychoanalytic thinking in a weekly staff support group to reflect on a structural change to the service and on clinical practice. This enabled the nurses to discover a system of defences, partly reflecting that of the patient group, from anxieties relating to the care of challenging and seriously ill patients.

It is suggested that the systematic use of psychoanalytic thinking for the support, training and supervision of staff makes a valuable contribution to the development and maintenance of safe practice with a view to meeting the Department of Health guidelines for clinical governance to improve quality of service, safeguard standards of practice and create an environment in which a high level of clinical care can be achieved and maintained.

Karina Barone

Karina Barone

Rua Pedro Noel, 48
04703-030 — São Paulo — SP — BRASIL
e-mail: karina_barone@hotmail.com

Karina Barone  

In this paper I discuss the extent to which psychoanalytic theory on mourning, transitional phenomena and creativity may be helpful in shedding light on the processes of dealing with loss created by severe illnesses in childhood. Through the discussion of three clinical cases, I explore the extent to which psychotherapeutic work with seriously ill children in hospital can be of benefit to the patients and their families.

[[RunningHead]]

BOOK REVIEWS

Why We Lie. The Evolutionary Roots of Deception and the Unconscious Mind

by David Livingston Smith (St Martin’s Press, New York, 2004). $24.95.

It is not a new observation that human beings lie habitually. Chapter 1 of this excellent book is entitled ‘Natural-Born Liars’ and has an epigraph by Mark Twain: ‘Lying is universal – we all do it; we all must do it’ (p. 1). The author reminds us that stories of deceit are as old as the story of Adam and Eve and the serpent, and extend throughout culture from King Lear to Little Red Riding Hood. While psychological research may be unnecessary to confirm that people lie to each other, ‘To grapple with dishonesty, we have to open our eyes to some unpleasant truths’ (p. 12). While many of these ‘unpleasant truths’ will already be familiar to the readers of this journal, the careful construction of an argument from evolutionary first principles draws on research and clinical observation that will not be familiar and makes for a fascinating journey.

David Smith is a professor of philosophy and co-founder and director of the Institute for Cognitive Science and Evolutionary Psychology at the University of New England. His academic title suggests a breadth of knowledge and interests that soon becomes evident in his writing as he draws on a wide range of research in animal and human behaviour. He begins the book with a deception of his own. Describing a disturbing incident of deception and brutality in Ethiopia, he reveals that the protagonists are baboons. Lying and deception, Smith argues, are universal features of the interactions between all living things. The deception of the Venus fly-trap and the angler fish will be familiar to anyone who watches wildlife documentaries on television. At a molecular level, the HIV virus deceives the T-lymphocyte in the human immune system by using the cell’s own system of DNA transcription to reproduce itself. While few would argue that animals, plants and viruses are not fundamentally deceptive, it is more comfortable to subscribe to the view that in the case of humans, culture has usurped genetics as the chief determinant of behaviour.

[[RunningHead]]

In Pursuit of Psychic Change — the Betty Joseph Workshop edited by Edith Hargreaves and Arturo Varchevker. Published in association with the Institute of Psychoanalysis (Brunner-Routledge, Hove, 2004). 206 pp. £18.99.

Deborah Blessing

Deborah Blessing

e-mail: Dblessinglicsw@aol.com

Deborah Blessing  

One measure of Betty Joseph's contribution to the field lies in her influence on subsequent thinkers. This book, a collection of 11 papers written by current or past members of the Betty Joseph workshop, attests to that impact. The idea to produce this book came on the heels of the June 1998 day of lectures in honour of Betty Joseph's 80th birthday and her contributions to psychoanalysis. Many of her central themes serve as backdrop; the homage paid to her is manifested by how deeply these ideas have taken root in the imagination of the next generations of analysts. However, it is not only the richness of her contributions that is celebrated in this volume. The workshop itself is showcased as a remarkably creative crucible in which discussions of clinical work, a spirit of inquiry and a containing atmosphere coalesced and synergistically produced a wealth of important ideas and refinements of technique.

The book is an ambitious project. One of its major aims is to convey the cross-fertilization of ideas and the sustained efforts to better understand ‘difficult to reach’ patients. As the book title suggests, the contents are organized around the obstacles to psychic change and the pressure to maintain psychic equilibrium. Each chapter illustrates the individual trajectory its author has taken in forging his/her own ideas about what stands in the way of development. Short comments by one or two other members of the workshop follow each chapter, which address enactments or stalemates in the data presented and highlight a shared emphasis on countertransference phenomena.

Hargreaves and Varchevker's excellent introduction provides an essential context for those not already well acquainted with Betty Joseph's work, or are unfamiliar with the workshop. It offers a glimpse into the more than 40-year history of the workshop chaired by Betty Joseph which began in 1962 as a postgraduate clinical seminar and later evolved into an ongoing workshop for senior analysts with a sustained membership. The list of workshop contributors includes highly respected analysts who have shaped theory and practice in their own right.

Three Faces of Mourning: Melancholia, Manic Defense and Moving on edited by Salman Akhtar (Jason Aronson, Northvale, NJ, 2002). 234 pp. US$ 40 hardback.

Maria Podlejska-Eyres

Maria Podlejska-Eyres

120 Belsize Lane
London NW3 5BA
e-mail: maria@eyres.org

Maria Podlejska-Eyres  

The very first impression of this book is the abundance of the letter ‘M’ on its cover; together with the Margaret S. Mahler Symposium logo depicting a woman cradling a baby, it brings almost immediate association with mothering and the strangely satisfying rhythm of its title brought to mind the soothing pace of nursery rhymes. Put it together with the book's provenance and it all certainly makes one curious. The ten-chapter volume consists mostly of papers presented at the 32nd Annual Margaret S. Mahler Symposium on Child Development in Philadelphia in 2001. It is a collaboration of nine, mainly Philadelphia-based, analysts led by Professor Salman Akhtar, a distinguished training and supervising analyst and a prolific writer/author in his own right who edited it. The book is touchingly dedicated ‘To the memory of Margaret S. Mahler. Teacher, friend, source of inspiration’. It is arranged in the form of a dialogue; four original papers, each followed by a response from the fellow analyst; the book begins with an opening chapter and ends with an overview.

The opening chapter, written by Henri Parens, attempts to give the reader a very brief synopsis of analytical thinking about mourning on three pages. Klein is dealt with in one sentence which, in a book that uses one of Klein's fundamental ideas in its title, is perhaps a little surprising. He then takes us through the four main papers, including one of his own. I found myself wondering about the chapter's usefulness; it did not seem to contain anything fresh to those familiar with the subject, while those relatively new to it will find it very sketchy to say the least. Somehow the feeling is that Parens says everything that he attempts to say in the name of his introductory chapter alone, ‘We all mourn; C'est condition humane’.

Helen Myers in her wonderfully titled chapter ‘Does Mourning Become Electra?’ moves with ease from Freud to Bowlby to arrive at the first of her questions about the loss of the mother. Is there something fundamentally unusual about it, especially for women who have not experienced loss beforehand? Is there a difference between how a man and a woman deal with it?

[[RunningHead]]

SEXUAL ATTACHMENTS: A THEORETICAL PSYCHOANALYTIC PERSPECTIVE ON INCEST

Chris Theunissen

Chris Theunissen

Chris Theunissen  

A psychoanalytic framework for understanding the incest-spectrum group of disorders is outlined. Incest disorders are viewed as a group of perverse activities representing a dynamic solution to developmental conflict or, alternately, as compensatory enactments that serve the function of re-regulating a defective or fragmenting self-structure. While incest is typically thought of as behavioural in nature, it also involves particular psychodynamics that underpin the sexualized behavioural enactment. Incestuous activity employs sexualized, bodily engagements to restore self-regulation or to regulate intense adverse affective states. This perspective emphasizes the need to understand the developmental and psychostructural features of the incest patient. Case material is provided to illuminate aspects of this understanding.

THE MEANING AND FUNCTION OF SILENCE IN PSYCHOTHERAPY WITH PARTICULAR REFERENCE TO A THERAPEUTIC COMMUNITY TREATMENT PROGRAMME*

John Gale and Beatriz Sanchez

Beatriz Sanchez

Beatriz Sanchez  

Psychoanalysis has understood silence either as a defence, a symptom or as a necessary punctuation, but it is more intensely a part of language than these explanations would suggest. This paper aims to show that philosophy may supply a more adequate understanding of silence in clinical practice than has hitherto been possible. Without philosophy, psychoanalysis can given no very coherent account of silence. Contemporary attempts in psychotherapy to reinstate a value to silence have misunderstood it and imbued it with quasi mystical qualities.

By putting silence back within the orbit of language this paper avoids the categorical mistakes in our understanding of this phenomenon, which are common in the literature. Far from being an impediment to psychotherapy, silence has a significance and value which make it an essential ally of speech in the therapeutic process. This has particular implications in a therapeutic community, where there is the possibility to develop quiet areas in the unit which can form part of the treatment environment and serve to provoke a reparative introspection.

Anne Ward  

This paper offers a spectrum of cases, in which the mother's ability to think about painful issues shaded from sensitive to near psychotic, and in which the outcome for the infant varied accordingly. Where the mother is free to contemplate her own thoughts, the infant is relatively uncontaminated by her projections and distortions, and she can be curious about its mind. Where defensive structures predominate, however, the infant becomes enmeshed by virtue of its unavoidable and persistent intrusions into the mother. The vignettes illustrate the interplay between the mother's mindfulness of her infant, and the infant's actual physical survival. The paper is not intended to ‘prove’ the argument, which has in any case been more rigorously developed by developmental psychologists, rather it offers a clinician's experience of its veracity, adducing extreme examples, and the clinical corollary that the mother-infant mind can be a vitally important focus. The therapist experiences something of this system, either by noticing it directly and allowing it to impinge, or by interacting with the mother and noting the counter- transference. By offering a mind that can bear to think about the experience, she is a thinking mother that metabolizes and returns it in a manageable form. The paper also highlights how ideas from the attachment literature may complement and enhance a more traditional psychoanalytic perspective.

[[RunningHead]]

Review: Masud Khan: the Myth and the Reality by Roger Willoughby with Foreword by Pearl King (Free Association Books, London, 2005). 320 pp. Hardback, £25.

Judith Issroff

Judith Issroff

27a Hampstead Hill Gardens, London NW3 2PJ
e-mail: judith_issroff@madasafish.com

Judith Issroff  

The blotchy, blurry cover reflects the blotched, blurry life of Masud Khan, subject of this studious, thoughtful biography. Roger Willoughby unveils some truths from the posturings, confabulations and actual contributions to psychoanalysis of the multi-layered personalities of a mis-fit whose life, he once suggested to me, aspired only to the truths of literature — perhaps his only enduring love and legacy. Khan was quite frank about having no need for any other kind of truth, rationalizing, ‘all fictions are true for the person who fabricates them, since they reflect an inner necessity’ (p. 243).

One of the clearest things to emerge from this overview of one of the most troubled, troubling and disreputable of individuals in the history of psychoanalysis is just what a gifted whopper of a whopper-teller he was; and how this demeaned the genuinely important work that he did (Hanly 1971).

Anaïs Nin wrote: ‘My diary is a mirror telling the story of a dreamer who, a long long time ago went through life the way one reads a book’. Even Khan's Work Books [modelled on Anaïs Nin's Diaries, 1931-1974
